play.description: Denver's three-time MVP continues to pair his unmatched court vision with elite scoring. With the game extending to overtime against San Antonio, Jokić uses his patented Sombor Shuffle to create space in the paint and rises for an unbelievable mid-range jumper over the outstretched Inspector Gadget arms of Victor Wembanyama that finds nothing but net. The veteran center's clutch shot helped snap the Spurs' impressive 11-game winning streak in a thriller that showcased his complete skill set alongside 40 points and 13 assists.

The reigning MVP's court vision knows no bounds, even when he's not the one scoring. With Denver cruising against their opponent on December 18, 2025, Jokić spotted Jamal Murray filling the left side midway through the third quarter. After going between his legs in traffic, the Serbian center delivered a pinpoint behind the back pass that Murray converted into a smooth turnaround hook shot from close range, pushing the Nuggets' lead to 18 points. Jokić finished with a near triple-double: 23 points, 13 assists, and 11 rebounds in the 126-115 victory.

No matter who was guarding him, Nikola Jokić showed the Phoenix Suns that he’s still got another level to reach. After a long regular season of consistent brilliance, the two-time MVP dominated Game 4 of the Western Conference semifinals. Jokić got the best of Kevin Durant on a drive to the basket as well as a catch-and-shoot 3-pointer, and when the Suns sent Deandre Ayton or Jock Landale to try to slow him down, the Joker beat them too with a post-up from the low block and relentless drive in traffic. Jokić converted difficult floaters and even got a desperation shot from the foul line over Devin Booker to fall while absorbing a foul. No matter what the defense threw at him, Jokić had an answer, exploding for a career-high and franchise-record 53 points on 20 of 30 from the field and 11 for 13 at the foul line. An All-Star five straight years, Jokić added four rebounds and 11 assists on May 7, 2023.

Few seven-footers possess the shooting touch to attempt fadeaway threes, but Nikola Jokić makes the impossible look routine. Early in Denver's dominant home victory, the Serbian maestro backed down his defender on the right wing before spinning into his signature turnaround motion. Despite the high degree of difficulty from 23 feet out, Jokić's fadeaway jumper found nothing but net with 10:11 left in the opening quarter, giving Denver an early 5-2 advantage. The reigning Finals MVP poured in 30 points with 12 rebounds and 6 assists as the Nuggets cruised to a 103-84 victory over Boston on February 25, 2026.

The three-time MVP's passing wizardry remains unmatched, even in crunch time when defenses key in on his every move. With Denver trailing Memphis by nine in the final minutes at FedExForum, Jokić surveys the floor from the high post and spots a cutting Peyton Watson. The Serbian center fires a pinpoint bounce pass behind his back through traffic, hitting Watson in stride for an easy finish at the rim with 1:04 left in regulation. Jokić notched his 16th assist en route to a 17-point, 16-assist, 10-rebound triple-double on November 24, 2025.

On a bingo board of phrases a team might use to evaluate a 137-point showing, “ugly win” likely wouldn’t make the cut. Especially if one took into account the types of passes that the all-seeing Nikola Jokić delivered to cutters across the Philadelphia 76ers’ floor on January 31, 2025. Unquestioned already in regards to his Most Valuable Player credentials, the Denver Nuggets’ superstar lived up to basketball’s great three-word distinction once more, punishing Philadelphia’s any and every thought to overhelp. First came an over-the-shoulder, no-look room service dime to his longtime running mate to set up a score, followed by a one-handed skip pass, guiding a different teammate’s path shortly thereafter. Equally sensational when toggling into must-score mode, Jokić put an offensive heatwave on sizzle in the final period, using a 13-point, two-triple final quarter to help Denver survive a high-scoring fireworks show down the stretch. Mile High’s newly-named seven-time All-Star was in his usual Hall of Fame-bound form, as the scoreboard confirmed through 28 points (on 11-of-16 shooting), joined by nine rebounds, 13 assists, two steals, and two 3-pointers.

Before some fans could even begin buffering the highlights of his 56-point, 16-rebound, eight-assist masterpiece from the previous night, Nikola Jokić — as generationally-great talents are wont to do — was already clocked in, collecting a performance of near-equal brilliance. Settled into a heatwave that, both on-court and statistically, seemed to know no bounds, the Denver Nuggets’ superstar center tests the could-be limits, collecting an end-of-half inbounds pass before inching as closely as possible to a reasonable buzzer-beating heave. Setting up camp around the Atlanta Hawks’ midcourt logo, Jokić, with Clint Capela’s defensive reach adding to the list of obstacles, calmly elevates, burying a 40-foot rainbow to close out a 25-point opening half. Jokić’s urgent, unforgettable play on December 8, 2024 ultimately yielded fruitful results, as the offense assembled both 43 assists and a wire-to-wire victory. Otherworldly as usual, the Serbian center recorded a double take-worthy 48 points (on 17-of-29 shooting), 14 rebounds, eight assists, three steals, three 3-pointers, and a +25 plus-minus. It allowed for NBA history’s first-ever occurrence of a player accumulating 100+ points, 30+ rebounds, and 15+ assists over a 48-hour span.

Thinking aloud through a list of things that could hypothetically help him conquer the Minnesota Timberwolves’ No. 1-ranked defense, Nikola Jokić settled on the idea that he wouldn’t mind having a duplicate clone of himself. One week later, with the series tied on his home floor, the soon-to-be three-time Most Valuable Player found that a vintage performance —  predictably built with a jaw-dropping box score — worked just as well. All smiles on a night that began with the raising of yet another trophy signifying his brilliance, the Denver Nuggets superstar supported it by raising his game, overwhelming Minnesota with all of his usual tricks. Some passes went behind his back; others traveled the length of the court. Some of his shots featured extravagant reverse-pivots, featuring just enough arc to surpass the reach of Rudy Gobert; others came along the perimeter. The end result, though, remained the same, as the Nuggets cruised to a convincing 3-2 series edge with a win on May 14, 2024. Bringing his A-game on the big stage, Jokić was magnificent, pouring in 40 points, seven rebounds, 13 assists, two steals, one block, and two 3-pointers along the way.

The three-time MVP showed he can still get up when given a runway, reminding everyone at Intuit Dome. Early in the first quarter against the Clippers, Christian Braun spotted Nikola Jokić sprinting down the middle in transition. The perfect pass found the Serbian center in stride, and Jokić rose up for a powerful running dunk that ignited the Nuggets bench. It was the start of a historic night as Jokić torched the Clippers for 55 points, 12 rebounds and 6 assists in Denver's 130-116 victory on November 12, 2025.

Back in action for the first time since winning the 2023 NBA championship, Nikola Jokić picked up right where he left off. As comfortable dominating in the paint as ever, Jokić tips an entry pass to himself, gathers possession then finishes with an untouchable push shot. Jokić finished the opening night rematch of last year’s Western Conference Finals with a 29-point, 13-rebound, 11-assist triple double. Jokić and the Nuggets received their rings and raised their banner prior to the October 24, 2023 contest.

Appropriately nicknamed for a player with a thousand tricks and counters up his sleeve on the offensive end, Nikola Jokić found that on December 3, 2024, against an equally-savvy Stephen Curry, that he, perhaps, had fooled even himself. Captaining the Nuggets’ transition charge with Denver trailing by a bucket in the final minute-and-a-half, the three-time Most Valuable Player accepts a high-profile challenge against Curry, pinballing into the Golden State Warriors’ star along the high post. Having commanded the NBA’s full respect as a passer, Jokić, conceivably by accident, uses it advantageously, selling the thought of a pass smoothly enough to turn his defender's stance in a full 360-degree angle before recognizing his opportunity at an unbothered, game-tying midrange bucket. Again living to his status as a man of no statistical “off nights,” Jokić’s box score brilliance enabled 38 points (on 14-of-24 shooting), 10 rebounds, six assists, five steals, one block, and three 3-pointers. The Denver Nuggets, team-wise, followed closely behind, collecting a come-from-behind NBA Cup win against West Group C’s eventual first-place finisher.

Despite a long season that has now stretched into the Western Conference Finals, Nikola Jokić’s legs — and his touch from long range — are only getting stronger. This spelled bad news for the Los Angeles Lakers, who couldn’t slow the two-time MVP down from inside or outside in Game 1 of the best-of-seven series. With the third-quarter clock hurtling toward zero, Jokić brought the ball up the court himself and beat the buzzer on a deep step-back 3-pointer even with Anthony Davis right in his grill on defense. Jokić was brilliant all night as he shot 12 for 17 from the field with a perfect 3 for 3 showing from behind the arc for a team-high 34 points while adding 21 rebounds and 14 assists for his sixth triple-double of a dominant postseason. The Joker has shot better than 50 percent from downtown through 43 playoff attempts, up from 38.3 percent during the regular season, as he led the Denver Nuggets to a 132-126 victory on May 16, 2023.

Among the many beautiful ironies of Nikola Jokić’s one-of-a-kind disposition is that, in a modern-day game influenced by efficiency, percentages, and gaudy statistics, nothing the Denver Nuggets’ superstar contributes ever comes across as forced or self-focused. January 23, 2025 served as Exhibit No. 1,000, as Jokić, solely focused on victory over the Sacramento Kings, accidentally stumbled into a never-before-seen statline in the NBA’s 79-year existence. Understandably, the most-discussed play arrived at the third quarter’s conclusion when Jokić — 66 feet away from the rim, and likely forgiven if he wanted to forgo a shot attempt — balanced into a quarterback-like stance before draining a buzzer-beater. What followed was a familiar-yet-feelgood scene: 19,676 collectively rising, celebrating the newest act of excellence from the newly-named seven-time All-Star. Contributions over the rest of Jokić’s 36-minute showing weren’t too shabby, as he unselfishly delivered his normal assortment of table-setting passes, catch-and-shoot triples, and ultimately, a fifth-straight triple-double. Assembling both history and victory at once, the sleeved Serbian superstar’s one-of-one box score ultimately read as follows: 35 points (on 12-of-19 shooting), 22 rebounds, 17 assists, one steal, two blocks, and two 3-pointers.

For a player already on a trajectory unlike any other over the NBA’s near-eight-decade-long history, it was, perhaps reasonable to wonder if Nikola Jokić could take his otherworldly game to even higher tiers in 2024-25. And yet, in between no-look passes and never-before-observed box scores, the three-time Most Valuable Player’s message was fittingly clear: just like the Cleveland Cavaliers on December 5, 2024, even basketball’s closest observers have seen nothing yet. Behind on the scoreboards, but predictably ahead in his processing, Jokić powers downfloor as the “point center,” selling the idea of a pass to the perimeter persuasively enough to freeze every blue jersey within view. Chessmaster-like in his ability to maneuver the floor’s pieces to his liking, Jokić concludes by uncorking a visionless dime to a cutting teammate, securing a highlight assist. As it often does, Jokić’s many highlights arrived alongside a share of history, even in December 5, 2024’s loss. In leaving behind a standout statistical tally —  27 points (on 13-of-26 shooting), 20 rebounds, 11 assists, and three steals — Jokić passed up a “Showtime” legend in purple-and-gold, with triple-double No. 139, third-most in league history.

When Nikola Jokić surveys the floor, every cutter becomes a potential scoring threat thanks to his otherworldly passing ability. Midway through the third quarter on November 28, 2025, the two-time MVP spotted Spencer Jones making a decisive cut through the lane. Jokić delivered one of his trademark no-look passes that hit Jones perfectly in stride, resulting in a powerful dunk that pushed Denver's lead to 14 points. The Serbian center tallied 21 points and 10 assists.

Denver Nuggets superstar Nikola Jokić needs only a quick moment to make a play - if you take your eyes off of him for one second, he’ll make you pay. Jokić makes a full pivot on the perimeter, then stops and spots a cutting LeBron James for a reverse, alley-oop flush. In his fourth All-Star appearance, Jokić had an impressive line of 10 points, nine rebounds and eight assists in Team LeBron’s 163-160 win over Team Durant on February 20, 2022.

On a night that would ultimately be defined by accuracy and precision in everything he attempted, Nikola Jokić, in his own fearlessly-unique way, decided to test the depths of even his own greatness. Coalescing his creativity, once-in-a-generation passing gifts, and photographic memory into a single breathtaking sequence, the Denver Nuggets superstar hauls in a rebound, pivoting into an outlet passing position in a single motion. As if allowing his intrusive thoughts to win, Jokić opts for the unthinkable, zipping a perfectly-placed pass the entire length of the floor, out of reach of every Washington Wizards defender, and into the outstretched arms of his teammate to set up an otherworldly transition score. The coast-to-coast dime chronicled Jokić’s night effectively, as the three-time Most Valuable Player required just 10 shot attempts to coordinate a wire-to-wire win on February 22, 2024. Pinpoint throughout, Jokić tallied 21 points — on a perfect 100 percent shooting — along with 19 rebounds, 15 assists, and one steal, officially checking Washington off as the final team on his triple-double bingo board. It also made Jokić just NBA history’s third-ever player to produce a triple-double against every team.

It took a few seasons for Nikola Jokić to become a nightly triple-double threat, but even as a rookie the future MVP and NBA champion was beginning to flash his distinctly unique style. When Jokić misfired on a contested layup in the paint, he grabbed his own miss and badly fooled the Memphis Grizzlies defense by whipping a no-look behind-the-back pass to Kenneth Faried for a second-chance bucket. The Joker posted nine points, two rebounds, two assists and two steals in 14 minutes of action on January 21, 2016. The 41st overall pick of the 2015 NBA Draft finished his first professional season by averaging 10 points and seven rebounds across 80 games to earn a selection to the All-Rookie First Team.

Slow starts are meaningless as long as you can finish like Nikola Jokić. The two-time MVP shook off a cold night from the field by heating up when it mattered most in Game 5 of the First Round against the Minnesota Timberwolves. With his Denver Nuggets clinging to a two-point fourth quarter lead, the Joker received a pass in the lane from Jamal Murray and fooled both Anthony Edwards and Karl-Anthony Towns with a slight pump fake before finishing with an and-1 hook shot while drawing a foul on Towns. Jokić scored 20 of his 28 points in the second half and completed a triple-double with 17 rebounds and 12 assists in Denver’s 112-109 closeout victory on April 25, 2023 in the 4-1 series win, setting up a Western Conference Semifinals showdown with the Phoenix Suns.

Look no further than at Nikola Jokić for a crash course in how the NBA has evolved in the modern era. With time fast expiring in the third overtime period of a match between the Denver Nuggets and LA Clippers, the do-everything big man draws the attention of two defenders 25 feet away from the basket.  Despite the aggressive double-team, the 280-pound point center finds a passing lane and air mails a skip pass directly into the hands of Aaron Gordon in the far corner just in time for a game-winning triple on January 19, 2022.

Slower-paced, methodical offensive attacks and defensively-minded gameplans by opponents are, in theory at least, supposed to make producing never-before-seen box scores and highlights a more difficult process come playoff time. Seven postseasons into his journey as one of NBA history’s great high-pressure performers, Nikola Jokić continues leaving it abundantly clear that this memo wasn’t received. Teetering on the brink of a 3-1 series hole if the Denver Nuggets’ late-game execution falters, the three-time Most Valuable Player supplies balance through a tried-and-true approach, putting Ivica Zubac through every high-post pivot and pump fake possible. Ultimately finding enough of a crevice for his trademark “Sombor Shuffle,” Jokić cashes in from midrange, gifting Denver a two-point edge with 16 seconds remaining. A similarly-distinctive game-winning dunk by Jokić’s frontcourt running mate helped ensure a season-saving win, but extraordinary play could be found both individually and cohesively on April 26, 2025. Jokić’s latest must-see statline included 36 points (on 14-of-25 shooting), 21 rebounds, eight assists, two steals, one block, and three 3-pointers, predictably the first of its kind on the playoff stage.

Nikola Jokić made basketball history with the first-ever 30-20-20 game in NBA history, dropping 31 points, 21 rebounds, and 22 assists in an overtime victory over the Phoenix Suns. The performance represented the pinnacle of all-around excellence, as the three-time MVP showcased every skill in his vast repertoire in one unforgettable night. The historic stat line demonstrated exactly why Jokić continues to redefine what's possible from the center position.
The Serbian maestro's unprecedented achievement came during a remarkable season that saw him earn All-NBA First Team honors for the fifth time. His ability to dominate scoring, rebounding, and playmaking at the highest level in a single game speaks to his unique basketball genius and complete mastery of the sport. This moment will forever stand as a testament to Jokić's greatness and his place among the most versatile players in basketball history.

Statsheet stuffers and triple-double talliers could be found at every turn of the neck, as Wild West foes went play-for-play during December 16, 2024’s shootout. One could be rest assured, though, that few would handle the job as brilliantly as No. 15 of the Denver Nuggets could. Incorporating a sprinkle of defensive skill into this sequence, Nikola Jokić first sends out invitations to a “block party,” swatting away Malik Monk’s layup attempt before winning a crowded rebound battle. On to the next task before the Sacramento Kings can react, Jokić unleashes a skip-ahead pass, hitting a down-the-floor teammate for a Eurostep finish that pushes Denver’s lead to a dozen. Ready for the Kings’ inevitable comeback charge a few quarters later, Jokić blended clutch plays of every type into his highlight reel, as the Nuggets survived seven lead changes over the final minute-and-change for a victory. Big time in his box score contributions, as always, Jokić boarded Denver’s flight with 20 points, 14 rebounds, 13 assists, two steals, one block, and a 3-pointer, securing his season’s 10th triple-double.

Record a triple-double, win the game — it’s become that simple for Nikola Jokić and the Denver Nuggets. The New Orleans Pelicans are powerless to slow down the two-time reigning MVP as he chases down a loose ball and weaves his way through three defenders with a dazzlingly-deceptive drive before finishing with a layup. Jokić made 10 of his 14 shots for 26 points, 18 rebounds and 15 assists as the Nuggets improved to 16-0 on the season whenever he posts a triple-double, as Denver got by the Pelicans 122-113 on January 31, 2023.

For 99 percent of the NBA’s player pool, opposing defenses might consider it a “job well done” if a lockdown defensive stand forces a shot attempt with only a few milliseconds remaining on the 24-second play clock. During Game Five’s crunchtime stretch on May 13, 2025, the Oklahoma City Thunder, unfortunately, found themselves eye-to-eye with a superstar with VIP credentials to that “one percent club.” Author of more than a few improbable, “only-Jokić”-type shots on the postseason scene, the Denver Nuggets’ megastar stumbles into another, first pivoting back towards the floor’s middle with Chet Holmgren tightly attached. Left with no choice but to unleash a 27-foot “Sombor Shuffle”-type shot, Jokić connects, prompting a stunned groan from the Paycom Center crowd.  Most Valuable Players rise to the occasion in all-important games, and even in a loss, Jokić did just that, exploding for 44 points (on 17-of-25 shooting), 15 rebounds, five assists, two steals, and five 3-pointers. It marked just NBA history’s second-ever instance of a player reaching those points-rebounds-assists-triples thresholds in a playoff environment.

Even in the month of January, the reigning NBA Finals Most Valuable Player’s clutch gene remains locked into postseason form. One bucket away from escaping Indianapolis with a hard-fought road victory, Nikola Jokić remains equal parts composed and aggressive, ghosting a ball screen before popping out into the open space. Suddenly wide-open and afforded enough time to have a sandwich, Jokić eases his way towards the top of the key before offering up a cold-blooded 3-point bucket to put the game’s outcome on ice. Jokić was business as usual, crossing the Indiana Pacers off of his triple-double bingo board and leaving him with, at the time, only one team left to reach the statistical landmark against. A catalyst in the win over the Indiana Pacers on January 23, 2024, the Denver Nuggets superstar registered 31 points, 13 rebounds, 10 assists, one block, and three 3-pointers.

Nikola Jokić is a do-it-all-force in the middle for the Denver Nuggets, and the two-time MVP now has another milestone to check off his list. Against the Oklahoma City Thunder, the Joker was his normal versatile self, using his burly frame to muscle for rebounds, showcasing his unparalleled ability as a passing center and continuing to score when needed. With 15 points, 13 rebounds and 14 assists in the November 3, 2022 victory, Jokić racked up the 79th triple-double of his career, surpassing Wilt Chamberlain for the most all-time from the center position.

They say two eyes are better than one; when you can pass like Nikola Jokić, sometimes no eyes will suffice. Pressuring the defense with his go-to pick-and-roll partner, the two-time Most Valuable Player forces three defenders to collapse, needing only a quick glance before delivering a no-look, over-the-head dish to a cutting teammate to set up a slam. As consistently brilliant as they come, Jokić kept the Denver Nuggets’ offense humming with 27 points, 10 rebounds, 14 assists and a block, helping his team stave off the New Orleans Pelicans for a primetime win on January 12, 2024.

What a storybook season it was for two-time Denver Nuggets MVP Nikola Jokić. The Joker was the driving force behind Denver’s first championship, setting career-highs in field-goal percentage and assists per game while averaging 24.5 points, 11.8 rebounds and 9.8 assists across 69 games. His 29 regular season triple-doubles were 15 more than anyone else, and he posted 10 more in 20 playoff games where he again nearly averaged a triple-double. He now has 105 career regular season triple-doubles and another 16 in the postseason, already ranking him in the top-six all-time in both categories. The 2022-23 season marked Jokić’s fifth straight year on an All-NBA team — two on the Second Team and three on the First. While he fell just short of a third straight MVP award — finishing runner-up to Joel Embiid — Jokić made up for it by being named MVP of both the Western Conference and NBA Finals. During the regular season Jokić became the first player since 1968 to post a 35-point, 25-rebound, 10-assist game, as well as the third in NBA history to record a 40-15-15 game. In his eighth season, Jokić passed Alex English as Denver’s franchise leader in assists and also became the first player in NBA history to lead a postseason in total points, rebounds and assists.

78 seasons worth of high-level basketball would make it difficult for even the NBA’s greatest talents to construct the type of play that hasn’t been seen before; with one breathtaking display after another, though, Nikola Jokić continues putting both that theory and opposing defenses to the test. Mythical in his ability to see even the unseen angles across basketball’s 94-by-50 court setup, the two-time Most Valuable Player elevates intuition and awareness a few notches, galloping down the floor with his eye on a transition score. Fully aware of the outlet pass entering his vicinity, but oblivious to its precise location, Jokić decelerates slightly, allowing the ball to drop right in front of his stride before rendering Taurean Prince’s hustle foolish with a perfectly-placed volleyball tap pass into the hands of his teammate for a fastbreak score. The future Hall of Fame center kept the astonishing displays and gaudy statlines rolling during the Denver Nuggets’ loss to the Los Angeles Lakers in Game Four. Taking his do-it-all nature to an additional level, Jokić turned in 33 points, 14 rebounds, 14 assists, one steal, one rejection, and a pair of 3-pointers on April 27, 2024.

The Orlando Magic had their sights set on overtime in the Mile High City. Nikola Jokić had other plans. Following an Orlando free throw that tied the game, the two-time reigning MVP stationed himself at the top of the key and waited for his moment to arrive. When Jamal Murray hit Jokić with a pass just inside the arc, the Joker took one dribble backward and drilled the step-back triple over Franz Wagner just before the buzzer to deliver the Denver Nuggets their 13th consecutive home victory. Jokić tallied 17 points, 10 rebounds and 14 assists for his 12th triple-double of the season as the Nuggets prevailed 119-116 on January 15, 2023.

From the moment that Nikola Jokić steps onto the hardwood, two things immediately enter the realm of possibility: a never-before-seen statline and a high-IQ play to match. In taking the best that the testy Toronto Raptors had to offer, the two-time NBA Most Valuable Player presented a little bit of both, shuffling the deck as only “The Joker” can, with nifty dimes, effortless scoring at all three levels, and an impenetrable defensive approach along the way. Revered for his unselfish play, the Denver Nuggets superstar’s box score was equally charitable, as Jokić turned in 35 points, 17 rebounds, 12 assists, six steals, and two blocks, making him NBA history’s first-ever player to construct such a box score. Jokić’s comprehensive effort helped the Nuggets offset a 22-point deficit on the way to one of the season’s most thrilling wins on March 11, 2024.

In a generation of basketball fueled by hot takes, debates, and an undying search for the “next big thing,” Nikola Jokić’s game has springboarded to a stratosphere that simply couldn’t be overlooked nor disputed. Even with his peak powers perfectly aligning with one of the NBA’s most talent-heavy eras, the 2023-24 Most Valuable Player assembled a season that left few arguments regarding the Association’s alpha dog, as evidenced by earning 79 of the 99 first-place votes. Holding the answer key to any test, Jokić’s versatility over his ninth season introduced next-level exclusivity, as he became just the second-ever player to produce a 2,000-point, 900-rebound, 700-assist year. The self-proclaimed “freak of nature” also flew in rarefied air, as just the ninth-ever to earn three MVPs. Jokić’s one-of-a-kind vision oversaw the Denver Nuggets’ 57-win season with marks in points (26.4), rebounds (12.4), assists (9.0), efficiency (61.2 eFG%), stocks (steals + blocks, 176, No. 14), and on-off (+11.6) that rivaled the Mile High altitude. Authoring one of basketball’s greatest underdog résumés, Jokić continues verifying one thought: the second-rounder may have been drafted during a fast-food commercial, but with a defender in his way, breakfast belongs to him, alone.

Whether he’s scoring points in bunches or using his gravity to set up an open teammate, Denver Nuggets big Nikola Jokić has the code to crack any defense. With the Nuggets needing one more bucket to seal the game and extend their season, the reigning MVP rolls after a screen, receives a pass and goes straight into a Eurostep before delivering an on-the-money dime to a wide-open Will Barton in the corner. Jokić put on a show for the Denver crowd with 37 points, eight rebounds, six assists and three triples in a 126-121 win over the Golden State Warriors in Game 4 of the First Round on April 24, 2022.

The Golden State Warriors have seen their fair share of 40-foot, game-winning daggers in front of the home crowd from a former NBA Most Valuable Player; Nikola Jokić simply made sure it wasn’t the one they expected. One assist shy of another triple-double, the Denver Nuggets superstar decided that overtime wasn’t in his plans, catching an inbounds pass with four seconds remaining before getting just close enough to launch a contested, 39-foot rainbow off the glass for an exhilarating victory. Sharing the floor with multiple stars, the Nuggets’ transcendent big made his case as the game’s best on January 4, 2024, tossing in 34 points, nine rebounds, 10 assists, two steals, and a pair of 3-pointers.

Through a humility and self-deprecating humor that holds true to this day, Nikola Jokić —  during a pre-NBA self-assessment in 2014 —  famously labeled himself as the type of player who doesn’t “run fast,” nor “jump high.” As his lightning-quick year-by-year trajectory and ascension towards the highest of heights in the NBA’s history books would someday attest, such perceived shortcomings never stood a chance in slowing him down, even from the beginning. Having already drawn scouts' eyes after showcasing potential during Summer League play, the Denver Nuggets’ soon-to-be-superstar requests a post-up against Montrezl Harrell, in hopes of a first-ever NBA bucket. Bringing signature footwork to the end-of-game sequence, Jokić, following a well-sold fake spin, turns over his right shoulder before connecting on a soft touch hook shot to formally introduce himself to the regular-season’s record books. Jokić’s small, two-point, four-minute role ultimately helped Denver crash the party on the Houston Rockets’ home opener on October 28, 2015 through a 20-point win. Finding his role wisely increased thereafter, the Serbian talent tallied a third-place finish in Rookie of the Year voting, immediately sprinkling in brief glimpses of a player who would soon sit among the Association’s all-time elites just a decade later.

Before he was an NBA champion and two-time league MVP, Nikola Jokić was hard at work becoming a triple-double savant. Although he had yet to begin his reign as annual league leader in triple-doubles, the Joker was starting to show flashes of his all-around brilliance on a nightly basis. Against the Milwaukee Bucks in his third season, Jokić did his standard little bit of everything, but what stuck out was the speed in which he compiled his 10th triple-double of the season. Jokić’s versatility was on full display in the first half as he connected on a midrange pull-up and a catch-and-shoot 3-pointer while also spinning his way to a driving layup in traffic. He facilitated for his teammates in a variety of ways, whipping a pass down low for an easy layup, dishing a swing pass for an open triple and making a no-look, behind-the-back pass look effortless. Just over a year after recording his first career triple-double in Milwaukee, Jokić logged the fastest triple-double in NBA history, achieving the feat with 1:54 to go before halftime in 14 minutes, 33 seconds of game time. He broke the previous record of 17 minutes that had stood since 1955, and ended up with 30 points, 15 rebounds and 17 assists in Denver’s 134-123 win on February 15, 2018.

Nikola Jokić finished as MVP runner-up after delivering one of the greatest individual seasons in NBA history, averaging 29.6 points, 12.7 rebounds, and 10.2 assists while becoming just the third player ever to average a triple-double. The three-time MVP set new standards for excellence from the center position, recording multiple historic performances including the first 30-20-20 game in NBA history and a 61-point triple-double against Minnesota.
Jokić's statistical dominance throughout the season reminded everyone why he's considered one of the most complete players ever. The Serbian superstar's ability to impact every aspect of the game while maintaining incredible efficiency set him apart as a generational talent. His MVP finalist recognition celebrates another remarkable campaign from a player who continues to rewrite the record books and redefine greatness at the center position.

At his current pace, Nikola Jokić is making history every time he steps onto a basketball court. That was no different in Game 3 of the NBA Finals when the Denver Nuggets recaptured control of the series with the Joker’s 10th triple-double of a legendary postseason run. Jokić produced his typical output of difficult turnaround jumpers, using his strength to back down Kevin Love in the paint for a smooth baby hook and later beat the Miami Heat veteran with a foul-line fadeaway. Jokić mixed in several of his patented mind-bending passes, including wrapping one around Bam Adebayo to hit a cutting Christian Braun for a back door slam. The two-time MVP posted 32 points, 21 rebounds and 10 assists — the first 30-20-10 triple-double in Finals history — as the Nuggets secured a 109-94 victory on June 7, 2023 to take a 2-1 series lead.

On Christmas Day, Nikola Jokić delivered a performance for the ages that had even the most seasoned basketball fans in awe. With the game tied at 126 in the fourth quarter, the two-time MVP receives a feed from Jamal Murray and shows why he's the most skilled big man in basketball history - driving into the paint and releasing his patented floating jumper with perfect touch. After Denver blew a 15-point fourth-quarter lead, Jokić took over in overtime with an otherworldly display. He knocked down a catch-and-shoot triple to keep the Nuggets alive, then put the game away moments later with a hard drive into the paint, finishing with a trademark floater through traffic. The Serbian center's historic 18-point overtime eruption - an NBA record - capped off an astonishing 56 points, 16 rebounds and 15 assists as Denver stormed back with an 11-2 overtime run to complete the 142-138 victory on December 25, 2025.

Nikola Jokic can do it all on the offensive end. The Denver Nuggets All-Star bangs up against two-time Defensive Player of the Year Rudy Gobert as he gets himself into position, then gracefully spins past the Utah Jazz center and finishes the play with an acrobatic reverse layup on January 31, 2021.

Even if you knew what Nikola Jokic was about to do next, good luck trying to stop it. The bruising Denver Nuggets center works against his defender with his back to the basket then pivots into a hook shot in a 50-point showing against the Sacramento Kings on February 6, 2021.

When you’re hot, you’re hot! With just 2 seconds left on the shot clock, Denver Nuggets star big man Nikola Jokić nails a deep off-balance three ball against the LA Clippers during Game 6 of their intense Western Conference Semifinal matchup on September 13, 2020.

To the rack with ease! In a rematch of last season’s thrilling Western Conference Semifinals series, superstar center Nikola Jokić of the Denver Nuggets gets free in the post, catches the sweet entry pass, and drops the easy jam against the LA Clippers on December 25, 2020. Jokić was terrific in the Christmas Day game, scoring 24 points, dishing out 10 dimes, and grabbing 9 rebounds.

Nikola Jokić has established himself as one of the most dominant players in the NBA. The reigning MVP works in the post before dishing a no-look pass to teammate Aaron Gordon for an elbow 3-Pointer in a win against the San Antonio Spurs on December 11, 2021. Jokić finished the game with 35 points, 17 rebounds, eight assists, one steal and two blocks.

It’s not just the vision that sets Denver Nuggets center Nikola Jokic apart, it’s the skill to execute his ideas. Jokic makes eye contact with a streaking JaMychal Green then laces a perfectly spun, one-handed bounce pass up the court without breaking stride. Green finished with an open flush against the Minnesota Timberwolves on January 5, 2021.

With the fancy footwork! Nikola Jokić of the Denver Nuggets shows off his beautiful big man moves before draining the rainbow jumper against the Los Angeles Lakers during Game 3 of their Western Conference Finals series on September 22, 2020.
Those handles! Nikola Jokić of the Denver Nuggets goes behind the back not once, but twice, on his way to the hoop for an easy layup against the Sacramento Kings on December 29, 2019.

Creative playmakers live to set up others but that doesn’t mean they can’t get their own buckets, too. Denver Nuggets center Nikola Jokic catches a pass as he trails up the floor then lobs a pass off the backboard to himself for an uncontested alley-oop lay-in against the Phoenix Suns in Game 1 of the Western Conference Semifinals on June 7, 2021.

With his 6-foot-11 frame and impeccable vision, Denver Nuggets center Nikola Jokić sees everything on the basketball court. That includes the defensive end, where the 2020-21 NBA MVP shows brilliance rotating over for a game-winning, no-look block in a 95-94 win over the Houston Rockets. The Joker did it all on both ends, contributing 28 points and 14 rebounds in the victory on November 6, 2021.
Those handles! Nikola Jokić of the Denver Nuggets goes behind the back not once, but twice, on his way to the hoop for an easy layup against the Sacramento Kings on December 29, 2019.

Nikola Jokic’s vision is unmatched. The Denver Nuggets center rolls to the basket after setting a screen for Jamal Murray, extends up to get a hand on a lob pass then deflects the ball to a wide open Paul Millsap for an easy two against the Phoenix Suns on January 22, 2021.

It’s post up time with Mr. Jokić! Denver Nuggets star big man Nikola Jokić flexes some fancy footwork in the key before draining a sweet rainbow jumper against playoff rivals the Utah Jazz on September 1, 2020.
Those handles! Nikola Jokić of the Denver Nuggets goes behind the back not once, but twice, on his way to the hoop for an easy layup against the Sacramento Kings on December 29, 2019.
